coredata.dc:title: The impressive effect of eco-friendly carbon dots on improving the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Behzad
Irannejad, Neda
Ensafi, Ali A.
Kazemifard, Nafiseh
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel and green strategy based on hydrothermal treatment was reported to synthesize highly water-soluble carbon dots from Rosemary leaves. A one-step, effective, low-cost and environmentally friendly method was used for the CDs preparation. Enhanced photoconversion efficiency of the TiO2-based dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC), is observed in the presence of carbon dots. Though recombination processes are generally inappropriate for the power conversion of DSSC, the photoanode, which was constructed from CDs/TiO2 represented a significant increase in the power conversion efficiency compared to the pure TiO2. In the novel designed DSSC the power conversion efficiency was enhanced. This enhancement was occurred due to the improvement in the connection between the nanoparticles that leading to ameliorate the electron hopping at the nanoparticle’s boundaries and efficient charge carrier separations. A significant improvement in all cell parameters in the presence of CDs (5%)/TiO2 as a photoanode was observed. The improvement of Jsc. (=16.94 mA cm2) and Voc. (=0.788 mV) resulted a 2.2-fold increase in the power conversion efficiency compared to TiO2-based DSSC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rosemary leaves
Green synthesized
Photoanode
Electrophoretic deposition
Dye-sensitized solar cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EPD procedure for fabrication of DSSC photoanodes
Effect of different parameters on the stability and fluorescence intensity of the CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Optimization of CDs synthesis conditions (A) Effect of time on the carbonization of 1.0 g of Rosemary leaves (at 100 mL) at 180 °C. (B) Effect of temperature on the carbonization of 1.0 g of Rosemary leaves (at 100 mL) for 12 h.

(A) Characterization of the synthesized CDs: (A) DLS diagram, (B) TEM image, (C) (I) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and (II) FL spectrum, (D) FT-IR spectrum, (E) X-ray diffraction pattern, and (F) EDX analysis of the CDs.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, (B) Effect of excitation wavelength (λex) on the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs, (C) Effect of solvent on the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs.

(A) J–V curves, (B) OCVD plot and (C) Electron lifetime of DSSC with the (a) TiO2, (b) CDs (1%)/TiO2, (c) CDs (2%)/TiO2, (d) CDs (3%)/TiO2, (e) CDs (4%)/TiO2, (f) CDs (5%)/TiO2, (g) CDs (6%)/TiO2 as a photoanode.
Mott-Schottky plot of photoanodes made based on the (a) TiO2, (b) CDs (1%)/TiO2, (c) CDs (2%)/TiO2, (d) CDs (3%)/TiO2, (e) CDs (4%)/TiO2, (f) CDs (5%)/TiO2.
(A) EIS plot and (B) IMPS plot of DSSC with the (a) TiO2, (b) CDs (1%)/TiO2, (c) CDs (2%)/TiO2, (d) CDs (3%)/TiO2, (e) CDs (4%)/TiO2, (f) CDs (5%)/TiO2 as a photoanode.
Schematic representation of EPD procedure and fabrication of DSSC.
Photovoltaic characteristics of DSSCs made based on TiO2 and CDs modified TiO2 photoanodes.
Donor density and flat band potential of DSSCs made based on TiO2 and modified TiO2 with different percentage of CDs as photoanodes, obtained from Mott–Schottky plots.

head.ce:title: The impressive effect of eco-friendly carbon dots on improving the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel and green strategy based on hydrothermal treatment was reported to synthesize highly water-soluble carbon dots from Rosemary leaves. A one-step, effective, low-cost and environmentally friendly method was used for the CDs preparation. Enhanced photoconversion efficiency of the TiO2-based dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC), is observed in the presence of carbon dots. Though recombination processes are generally inappropriate for the power conversion of DSSC, the photoanode, which was constructed from CDs/TiO2 represented a significant increase in the power conversion efficiency compared to the pure TiO2. In the novel designed DSSC the power conversion efficiency was enhanced. This enhancement was occurred due to the improvement in the connection between the nanoparticles that leading to ameliorate the electron hopping at the nanoparticle’s boundaries and efficient charge carrier separations. A significant improvement in all cell parameters in the presence of CDs (5%)/TiO2 as a photoanode was observed. The improvement of Jsc. (=16.94 mA cm2) and Voc. (=0.788 mV) resulted a 2.2-fold increase in the power conversion efficiency compared to TiO2-based DSSC.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, due to environmental concerns, use of renewable energy sources have been an important issue. Herein, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCC) through the production of clean and renewable energy is one of the most promising candidates for photovoltaic systems (Tetreault et al., 2011).

These type of solar cells compared to the conventional silicon solar cells have attracted particular attention due to their unique features such as flexibility, low cost, and simplicity of their manufacture (Sensitizers et al., 2009). A typical DSSC is composed of a mesoporous oxide film (usually anatase TiO2) as the photoanode covered by a monolayer of photosensitizer molecules, an iodine-based electrolyte, and a Pt-coated counter electrode (Boro et al., 2018). Whereby the photoanode plays a very important role in improving the performance of DSSC. So far, a lot of scholars have focused on this topic (Fan et al., 2017). In DSSC, the overall cell efficiency strongly depends on different parameters such as the suitable binding dye molecules on the photoanode, power absorption in the visible region of the solar spectrum, and the more efficiently transfers of electrons compared with available recombination processes (Ye et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2012).
A photoanode with a high internal specific surface area ensures the high loading of sensitizer molecules and subsequently high visible light absorption (Ko et al., 2011).

On the other hand, there are different strategies, which can be effective ways to reduce the recombination processes. Some of them are including surface organic modification due to the effective charge separation and increasing the lifetime of electrons in semiconductors and another strategy is doping metal ions or nano-metals into the TiO2 lattice (Kumar and Devi, 2011).

Carbon dots (CDs) are newest branches of carbon nanomaterials (Jahanbakhshi and Habibi, 2016). Compared to quantum dots (such as CdTe, etc.) and organic dyes, CDs with good chemical inertness leads to low toxicity, are suitable for bioimaging and biomolecule/drug delivery (Sun et al., 2014). CDs in the size of less than 10 nm with a broad absorption spectrum, multiple exciton generations, high photostability, adequate fluorescent performance, high solubility in water and easy modification are known as one of the perfect dopants to improve the properties of TiO2 (Wang and Hu, 2014).

There are many different methods for synthesis of CDs such as laser ablation (Castro et al., 2016), hydrothermal (Hsu and Chang, 2012; Qu et al., 2012), microwave and ultrasonic (Ma et al., 2012). Among these methods, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment is a stable, one-step, rapid, effective, low cost, environmentally friendly, nonhazardous, and surface states tunable method. In hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, the size and nanostructure of prepared CDs are controllable. In recent years, preparation of CDs via green synthesis using raw carbon sources have been attracted a lot of attention. Green synthesis using natural carbon sources has many advantages including eco-friendly, non-expensive and simplicity of the procedure (Yu et al., 2015). Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis L.) is a subset of the mint family and an evergreen aromatic shrub that grows in many parts of the world (Jiang et al., 2011). It contains the main active constituents such as caffeic acid, rosmarinic acid, ursolic acid, carnosic acid, and carnosol (Ngo et al., 2011). Rosemary can be use as a natural carbon source for the formation of CDs.

Another important parameter to improve the performance of DSSC is designing a perfect uniform layer with full coverage of TiO2 on a fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate (Hamadanian et al., 2012). According to the results of previous research, pulsed electrophoretic deposition (EPD) method is especially attractive over the doctor blade method. This is due to creating better inter-particle connectivity, its short formation time, binder-free process and ability to the preparation of uniform coated layer (Rezaei et al., 2017).

Herein, a simple hydrothermal method for the green synthesis of water-soluble CDs has been reported using Rosemary leaves as a precursor. Afterwards, TiO2 photoanode was modified with the CDs and then it was applied in a DSSC. On the designed photoanode, the electron hopping at the boundaries improved in the presence of CDs caused by the interconnection of the TiO2 nanoparticles. In this case, the effective separation of charge carriers and increases the lifetime of the electrons are owing to the well-transferred electron to the conduction band of TiO2 and accumulated holes in the valence band of CDs. In the presence of CDs (5%)/TiO2 as a photoanode, the power conversion efficiency was 7.32% (Jsc. 16.94 mA cm2, Voc. 0.788 mV), which indicated a 125% increase in the conversion efficiency compared to those of TiO2 DSSC (Jsc. 7.92 mA cm2, Voc. 0.765 mV).

In order to synthesize aqueous soluble CDs via hydrothermal treatment, Rosemary leaves were collected from Isfahan University of Technology campus garden. Then, the collected Rosemary leaves were washed with distilled water, to remove any contaminants. After washing, the sample was dried at room temperature. The dried sample was ground and then 1.0 g of the ground Rosemary leaves was added to 100 mL of distilled water in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Then, the obtained solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 5 min) to separated unreacted components and the supernatant (containing CDs) was purified using a centrifugal filter. Then, it was kept in a dark glass container at 4.0 °C. For XRD, EDX and FT-IR spectroscopic analysis, the CDs were dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EPD procedure for fabrication of DSSC photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: Pulsed EPD method with regulated power supply (Isfahan-Tak Electronics) was used to provide a uniform coated layer on the surface of the cleaned FTO (Solaronix Co.). To apply pulsed direct current waveforms (100 Hz frequency), regulated power supply connecting with a function generator (GW-Instek GFG-2080H) and a signal amplifier device were used. In this case, to deposit a uniform film on the substrate, pulsed direct current by a peak to peak voltage of 6.0 V at 100 Hz was applied between the FTO glass (as an anode) and a stainless steel plate (as a cathode) with a distance of 1.0 cm, with a deposition time of 8 min. A 1.0 g L−1 colloidal suspension of TiO2 (P25, Degussa) was prepared in a mixture of ethanol/2-propanol solvents (1:4 v/v) containing 1.0 × 10−4 M magnesium nitrate. Separately, a suitable colloidal suspension of the CDs was prepared in the above solvents, which used to provide the TiO2 suspension. Both of the prepared suspensions were sonicated for one hour. Then, a certain portion of the CDs suspension (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 µL) was added to the TiO2 suspension to prepare mixtures with different amounts of the CDs. In order to individualize the kinds of films, photoanodes were named as CDs(1%)/TiO2, CDs(2%)/TiO2, CDs(3%)/TiO2, CDs(4%)/TiO2, CDs(5%)/TiO2, CDs(6%)/TiO2.

The following temperature program is offered to sinter the prepared photoanodes. At first, the fabricated photoanode films were heated at 120 °C in a muffle furnace for 15 min. Then, the temperature was increased up to 500 °C with a heating rate of 5.0 °C min−1 and was kept at this temperature for 30 min. In order to sensitize the photoanode, the prepared films (with active surface area of 0.25 cm2) were immersed in cis-di(isothiocyanate)-bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylate) ruthenium(II) bis(tetrabutylammonium) N719 ethanol solution (8.0 × 10−4 mol L−1) for 24 h. The counter electrode was covered with platinum by employing the electrochemical method. The Surlyn (Solaronix Co.) thermoplastic frame used to attached Pt-counter electrode with photoanode. Finally, the iodine-based electrolyte (Sharif solar Co.) was injected into the space between the photoanode and catode (Scheme 1 ).

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Jasco FP–750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). A double beam Jasco V–570 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with a 1.0-cm quartz cell was used to record the UV/Vis absorption spectrum. Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was applied to obtain the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (at 4000–400 cm−1, KBr plate). The diameter of the CDs, size distribution, and zeta potential were investigated using dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument, Malvern ZEN3600 A (UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-Advance Bruker using Cu-Kα radiation, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM30 300 kV, The Netherlands) were used for characterization of the CDs. The CDs were purified using a 10 kDa MW cut-off Amicon Ultra-4 centrifugal filter unit (Millipore, Madrid, Spain)

Photovoltaic performance of DSSCs that illuminated under AM 1.5 simulated sunlight (100 mW cm−2), was measured using a Biologic SP-300 potentiostat. In order to record the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) for DSSC at an open circuit voltage in 100 mW cm−2, Eco-Chemie Autolab PGSTAT 302 N electrochemical workstation with NOVA software was used in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz. The intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) measurement was performed by a diode laser light source, which its intensity is varied at a wavelength of 620 nm.

Time of carbonization and reaction temperature are two main variables in the synthesis of CDs via hydrothermal method. To achieve the maximum fluorescence intensity (F) of the CDs with a minimum full width at half maximum (FWHM) height (as a criterion of reaction progress), time and temperature were optimized to find the best value for these variables. With this aim, the carbonization of Rosemary leaves (1.0 g in 100 mL) at 180 °C was done at different times (0–14 h). As can be seen in Fig. 1 A, the F/FWHM ratio of the CDs increases with increasing the reaction time because the more amount of the precursor has been converted to CDs. Whereby the slope of the diagram has fallen at more than 12 h. So, in order to save energy, 12 h was considered as the optimal reaction time.

To optimize the reaction temperature, the carbonization of the nanoparticles was done at different temperatures (80–220 °C) for 12 h. The obtained results are given in Fig. 1B. According to the results, the best temperature for the carbonization of the precursor was 180 °C.

According to the obtained results from the DLS, the average size of the synthesized CDs was about 5 nm (Fig. 2 A). TEM image (Fig. 2B) was also used to determine the size and morphology of the synthesized CDs. As can be seen in this figure the diameter of the CDs was about 5 nm.

In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 2C(I)) two incorporated peaks of n–π* transition and π–π* transition gave a broad peak at 250–350 nm. Fig. 2C(II) displays a narrow fluorescence (FL) spectrum of the synthesized CDs.

Fig. 2D shows the FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized CDs. The band at 1600 cm−1 was related to the CO and CC stretching vibrations. The band at 1065 cm−1 was assigned to CO bending vibration. The absorption band centred at ∼3300 cm−1 was related to OH and NH stretching vibrations. The band at 2930 cm−1 appointed to the CH stretching mode. The peaks which, were placed at 1590 and 1400 cm−1 could be identified as the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, respectively. These results indicated that the hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine groups were present on the surface of the synthesized CDs (Mehta et al., 2015).
The XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 2E) displayed two broad peaks at 2θ = 22° (0 0 2) and 45° (1 0 0), which showed a graphitic-like aromatic structure of the CDs (Liu et al., 2017).

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was applied for the elemental analysis of the synthesized CDs. The obtained result (Fig. 2F) showed the presence of 66.1% C, 27.4% O, 1.4% Mg, 0.5% P, 0.6% S, 0.7% Cl, 1.9% K, 1.4% Ca.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parameters on the stability and fluorescence intensity of the CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of the CDs (90 μL in 2.0 mL of 4.0 mmol L1− universal buffer made from boric acid, acetic acid and phosphoric acid each 0.04 mol L−1) under the excitation wavelength (λex) of 400 nm was recorded at 488 nm (the wavelength of maximum fluorescence intensity). The obtained results (Fig. 3 A) indicates the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increases with increasing the solution pH, whereas at pHs ≥ 7 there were no significant changes in the fluorescence intensity. Presence of negative surface charges on the CDs, which was verified using the IR spectrum, is the main reason of this behavior.

The effect of λex on the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs (35 μL in 2.0 mL of 4.0 mmol L1− universal buffer) was studied too (Fig. 3B). As can be seen in this figure, the emission wavelength and the fluorescence intensity depend on λex. This phenomenon is due to the differently sized nanoparticles (a quantum effect) and/or different emissive traps on the surface of CDs or other causes are unknown so far (Wang and Hu, 2014).

With the aim of evaluation of the effect of solvent on the fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs (30 μL CDs in 2.0 mL of different solvents with different polarity) were recorded at a range of 420–600 nm (λex 400 nm). As can be seen in Fig. 3C, the CDs were more soluble in polar solvents such as ethanol. This is due to the presence of polar functional groups on the surface of the CDs.

The current density-voltage (J-V) measurements under the circumstances of full sunlight illumination (AM 1.5, 100 mW cm−2), were performed to evaluate the photovoltaic characteristics of DSSCs with a different weight ratio of the CDs decorated on TiO2 film as a photoanode (Fig. 4 A). The photovoltaic parameters of the fabricated DSSCs are summarized in Table 1 . The CDs in 5.0 nm dimensions with carboxylic groups on the surface were well bound to TiO2, consequently the connection between TiO2 nanoparticles improved. Under these conditions, the connection between the nanoparticles was improving the electron hopping at the nanoparticle boundaries, which ultimately leads to increase Jsc (Liu et al., 2016). Also creating a uniform coated layer on the FTO substrate and reducing the electrolyte contacts with FTO substrate, led to enhancing the recombination processes and increase the DSSC conversion efficiency. According to the obtained results, the values of short-circuit current (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF) and conversion efficiencies (ŋ) depend on the percentage of decorated CDs in the TiO2 photoanodes. By using CDs(5%)/TiO2 as a photoanode of DSSC compared to pure TiO2, a 125% increase in the conversion efficiency and a 2.2-times increase in the power conversion efficiency were achieved.

An increase in the Jsc is a direct consequence of the specific surface area enhancement in the presence of CDs, which leads to increase the adsorbed dyes on the photoanode. The most important factors that affect the conversion efficiency of solar cells are effective charge separation, increasing the rate of electron transfer, and reducing the recombination process. One of the major advantages of using synthesized CDs is a broad absorption range from ultraviolet to the near-IR region. Under these conditions, photoelectron injection into the TiO2 increases substantially and consequently the deep trap sites were filled. As a result of trap sites blocking, electrons do not feel the presence of these recombination centres during the passage of the conduction band and do not engage in the recombination processes anymore. Therefore, with increasing percentage of the CDs, the electron transfer rate (at a considerably shorter time) was increased and the Jsc significantly improved. So an effective separation of charge carriers and increase the lifetime of the electrons were owing to the well-transferred electron to the conduction band of TiO2 and accumulated holes in the valence band of CDs. Thus, increasing the CDs content on the modified CDs/TiO2 photoanode to 6.0% eventually leds to decreasing in the conversion efficiency (Table 1). It is necessary to mention that the efficiency of DSSC, which was made based on CDs(6%)/TiO2 (6.79%) represented higher value than that of DSSC made based on unmodified TiO2 nanoparticles (3.25%). In addition, it was lower than that of DSSC, which was made based on CDs(5%)/TiO2 (7.32%). This percentage of the CDs loading can act as a new recombination centre so, reducing the amount of the charge carrier led to decreasing in the Jsc. According to this fact, in the presence of the nanostructures CDs on CDs/TiO2 the Fermi level shifted to more high-level, and the Voc for CDs/TiO2 modified photoanodes increased compared to pure TiO2.

In order to achieve a deeper perspective on the operation of DSSCs, it is possible to use a series of resistance (RS) and shunt resistance (RSH) obtained from the J-V curves (Table 1). In an ideal solar cell, the RS tends to zero. RS relates to the electron transfer paths inside the solar cell (photovoltaic process). Therefore, less resistance expresses the improvement in the electron transfer paths and facilitates the passage of the charge carriers. In connection with RSH, it tends to be infinite under ideal conditions. RSH relates to the electrons, which are involved in non-photovoltaic processes, including recombination processes. Therefore, higher RSH means that the lower electrons are participating in the recombination processes, which is evidence of the improvement in the solar cell efficiency (Zhang et al., 2016). Based on the obtained data from the J-V curve, increasing in the percentage of the CDs as TiO2 modifier led to improving the charge transfers, reducing the recombination processes, decreasing in the RS, and increasing in the RSH.

To evaluate reduction in the recombination processes of electrons and increase the cell efficiency, open circuit voltage decay analysis was used. According to the following equation, the lifetime of electrons was obtained (Choi et al., 2013):
τ n
=
k B
T/e
(
dV oc
/ dt )
- 1
where the thermal energy and the positive elementary charge are shown with the kBT and e symbols, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 4B, with increasing the percentage of the CDs in the designing photoanodes, voltage drop occurred in a long time and the lifetime of the electrons increased. Reduction in the recombination processes in the CDs-based photoanodes can be attributed to increasing the effective transmission of electrons. On the other hand, in the presence of CDs as a modifier, with trap sites blocking and effective separation of charge carriers in the designed photoanode, the back recombination processes from the conduction band of TiO2 decreased, which led to increasing in the effective lifetime of the electrons (Fig. 4C).

The flat band potential (Efb) at the photoanode of DSSCs, is a highly effective parameter in determining the position of the Fermi level of TiO2. In order to determine the position of Efb, Mott-Schottky analysis was used (Kirchartz et al., 2012). Mott-Schottky analysis for TiO2 photoanodes and modified photoanode, by incorporating various percentages (1.0–5.0%) of the reformer CDs, are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2 . According to the Mott-Schottky equation, the density of the charge carriers (ND) and flat band potential (Efb) are calculated with gradient dCsc/dE and the intercept of the obtained line, respectively.
1
C
SC
2
=
2 ( E -
E
fb
- k T ) / e
e ε
ε 0
N D

In this equation, capacity space (Csc), dielectric constant of TiO2 and vacuum (ε and ε0), charge carrier density (ND), applied potential (E), Boltzmann's constant (k) and absolute temperature (T) are requirements. In Mott-Schottky curves, Efb transition towards more negative values, indicate an increase in the open circuit voltage of the designed photoanodes. As can be seen, with increasing the percentage of the CDs nanostructures in the CDs/TiO2, Voc increased. The flat-band potentials of TiO2 and different percentages (1.0–5.0%) of the CDs, as a modifier on TiO2, was estimated from experimental analysis, which were −0.72, −0.79, −0.89, −0.97, −1.02 and −1.12 V (vs. Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl), respectively. Under these conditions, by increasing the Efb, the difference between the Fermi level of the semiconductor and redox potential in the electrolyte was increased, which ultimately led to improving the efficiency of DSSCs. Increasing in Efb to higher values, following the increase in the percentage of CDs, proved that electron transfer through the the conduction band was facilitated in the presence of this modifier, the electron enrichment of the TiO2, and the trap sites blocking. On the other hand, increase in the density of the charge carriers improved the electron transfer and collection efficiency.

To achieve more comprehensive information about the dynamic of transfer/recombination processes of electrons in DSSCs, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique was used (Hoshikawa et al., 2005). The Nyquist plots in the frequency range of 10−2–105 Hz and a voltage equal to the Voc (at the radiation of mW cm−2) for each of the DSSCs are shown in Fig. 6 A. In this case, there is a semicircle in the middle frequencies of the Nyquist diagram, which attributed to the impedance data (Rct) of the TiO2/dye/electrolyte. Whereby in the presence of the CDs, the charge carriers separate effectively, the recombination process between TiO2, and electrolyte reduced. The decrease in the Rct (as a result of a decrease in semicircle radius) shows an impressive improvement in the charge transfer processes and the absence of recombination processes in the CDs modified photoanodes. This reduction in the Rct can be attributed to two fundamental parameters in the presence of CDs. (I) Good bonding of the CDs to TiO2 and improving connections between TiO2 nanoparticles, which prevent direct contact between the electrolytes and FTO substrate, eventually led to decreasing direct electron recombination process. (II) A significant increase in the internal specific surface area, led to significant increase in the absorbed N719 molecules. On the other hand, by utilizing the photon absorption capability of CDs, the number of injected photoelectrons into TiO2 was increased. Combination of these features has led to the blocking of trapping sites, and the reduction of recombination processes. So by increasing the percentage of the CDs in the CDs/TiO2, the electron transfer is accelerating and the charge transfer resistance is reduced, which results in an increase in Jsc. The obtained results are shown in Table 3 .

IMPS is a very accurate measurement analysis to study the kinetic of electron transfer under short circuit conditions (Qiao, 2017). In this analysis, DSSCs were irradiated by a diode laser source with variable intensities at 620 nm at a short-circuit conditions, so the electrons were injected into the semiconductor by the following mechanism of Diffusion-trapped transmittance. In this case, the illumination will be a sinusoidally modulated light, which has a small AC component that is 10% of its DC component. So, the applied sinusoidal radiation led to injecting the number of specific and additional electrons into the semiconductor. Given that the system is under short circuit conditions, the transfer of these additional electrons into the photoanode is evaluated. As a result, useful information can be obtained regarding the electron transfer time (τt). The τt was determined using the following equation: τt = 1/2πfIMPS, where fIMPS is the frequency made minimized IMPS imaginary components (Table 3). According to the results (Fig. 6B), the photoanode surface modification with nanocomposite of CDs/TiO2 lead to lower charge transfer resistance. By reducing the transmission time of electrons, the probability of electron-hole recombination reduced, which leads to an increase in the conversion efficiency of the DSSC. On the other hand, due to the effective separation of charge carriers in designing photoanode, the recombination processes, are reduced dramatically. The results obtained in this section have a good agreement with the results of Mott-Schottky, EIS, and Jsc.

In this work, a novel and green strategy to synthesize carbon dots from the Rosemary leaves, which is based on the hydrothermal treatment, is reported. Different methods such as FT-IR, DLS, TEM, XRD, EDX, UV–Vis absorption, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used for characterization of the synthesized CDs. This one-step synthesis method has some advantages such as rapid, effective, low cost, eco-friendly, non-expensive and ease of production. Herein, the CDs modified TiO2-based photoanode in DSSC could exhibit efficiencies up to 125% under one sun. In the newly designed photoanode by having a high specific surface area, the dye adsorption and photo-excited electrons generation, significantly improved. The major advantage of sin using the synthesized CDs is a broad absorption range from the ultraviolet to the near-IR region. Due to the efficient charge carrier separation and increase the lifetime of the electrons owe to the well-transferred electrons to the conduction band of TiO2 and accumulated holes in the valence band of CDs, the power conversion efficiency of the DSSCs increased significantly. The power conversion efficiency was 7.32% (Jsc. 16.94 mA cm2, Voc. 0.788 mV) which indicated a 2.2-times increase in the conversion efficiency compared to those of TiO2 DSSC (Jsc. 7.92 mA cm2, Voc. 0.765 mV).
The paper was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge, Isfahan University of Technology Research Council (IUT) for supporting this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano-structured TiO2/ZnO nanocomposite for dye-sensitized solar cells application: a review
